---
title: "Encrypted Mempool"
description: "Build, sign, and submit encrypted-mempool transactions with the Aptos TypeScript SDK."
sidebar:
  label: "Encrypted Mempool"
---

import { Aside, Steps } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

Encrypted-mempool transactions wrap your Move payload in a ciphertext at build time, so it stays encrypted while pending and is decrypted by validators only at execution. The flow is the same as a plaintext transaction — **build → (optional) simulate on a plaintext twin → sign → submit → wait** — with a few extra `options` passed to `aptos.transaction.build.*`.

<Aside type="note">
  **Encrypted mempool** is the protocol name ([AIP-144](https://github.com/aptos-foundation/AIPs/blob/main/aips/aip-144-encrypted-mempool.md)); some product material calls it **encrypted pending transactions** — same feature. See the [Encrypted Pending Transactions](/build/guides/encrypted-pending-transactions) guide for background on visibility.
</Aside>

## Prerequisites

When you set `encrypted: true`, you must also pass `authenticationKey` for the sender — either an `AccountPublicKey` (the SDK derives the auth key for you) or a raw 32-byte hex string. The network you submit to must support the feature; check the ledger info first:

```typescript
const ledgerInfo = await aptos.getLedgerInfo();
const supportsEncrypted = Boolean(ledgerInfo.encryption_key);
```

If the node doesn't expose `encryption_key`, building with `encrypted: true` throws.

## Build, sign, and submit

<Steps>
  1. Build with `encrypted: true` and `authenticationKey`.

     ```typescript filename="encrypted-transfer.ts"
     const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
       sender: sender.accountAddress,
       data: {
         function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
         functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
       },
       options: {
         encrypted: true,
         authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
       },
     });
     ```

  2. Sign and submit as usual.

     ```typescript filename="encrypted-transfer.ts"
     const pending = await aptos.signAndSubmitTransaction({
       signer: sender,
       transaction,
     });

     await aptos.waitForTransaction({ transactionHash: pending.hash });
     ```
</Steps>

<Aside type="note">
  Encrypted transactions pay a minimum gas-unit price of **200 Octas/gas-unit** (twice the network base minimum). The SDK enforces this floor at build time: prices below it are bumped automatically, and the SDK warns when a caller explicitly set a lower value.
</Aside>

## Simulating an encrypted transaction

`aptos.transaction.simulate.*` rejects encrypted payloads — simulation is not supported. Build a **plaintext twin** with the same `data`, simulate that, then build again with `encrypted: true` for submission:

1. `build.simple({ ..., options: { /* no encrypted */ } })` — same function, arguments, gas hints.
2. `aptos.transaction.simulate.simple({ ... })` — tune `maxGasAmount` / `gasUnitPrice` from the result.
3. `build.simple({ ..., options: { encrypted: true, authenticationKey, ...gas from step 2 } })` — submit this build.

See [Simulating Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/simulating-transactions) for the general simulation flow.

## Multi-agent

Pass `secondarySignerAuthenticationKeys` in the same order as `secondarySignerAddresses`. Each entry accepts `AccountPublicKey` or raw hex.

```typescript filename="encrypted-multi-agent.ts"
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.multiAgent({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  secondarySignerAddresses: [secondary.accountAddress],
  data: {
    function: "0x<module>::<contract>::<multi_agent_entry>",
    functionArguments: [/* ... */],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
    secondarySignerAuthenticationKeys: [secondary.publicKey],
  },
});
```

## Fee payer (sponsored)

When `feePayerAddress` is non-zero, also pass `feePayerAuthenticationKey`.

```typescript filename="encrypted-sponsored.ts"
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  withFeePayer: true,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
    feePayerAuthenticationKey: sponsor.publicKey,
  },
});
```

## Orderless

Pass `replayProtectionNonce` together with `encrypted: true`. The transaction uses orderless replay protection while the payload stays encrypted. See [Orderless Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/orderless-transactions).

```typescript filename="encrypted-orderless.ts"
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
    replayProtectionNonce: 12345n,
  },
});
```

## `claimedEntryFunction` (optional)

The SDK handles this automatically for **sponsored or multisig** builds — you typically never set it. The one reason to override is when you want to expose only the module name to pre-decryption observers (sponsor, multisig owner, transaction filters) and keep the function name hidden until decryption:

```typescript
options: {
  encrypted: true,
  authenticationKey: sender.publicKey,
  feePayerAuthenticationKey: sponsor.publicKey,
  claimedEntryFunction: {
    module: "0x1::aptos_account",
    // functionName intentionally omitted
  },
};
```

When you do set `functionName`, it must match the real entry function exactly.

## Caveats

- **Keyless signers are not supported.** Encrypted transactions cannot be signed by keyless or federated keyless accounts — the build/submit will throw before contacting the node.
- **Encrypted multisig is feature-gated** on some networks; submission may be rejected even when the SDK build succeeds. Watch your network's release notes.

## Further reading

- **[Encrypted Pending Transactions guide](/build/guides/encrypted-pending-transactions)** — product overview, visibility, AIP-144 spec, and cryptography paper.
- **[Aptos batch encryption (cryptography)](https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-core/tree/main/crates/aptos-batch-encryption)** — `aptos-core` implementation detail (optional reading).
